value: Headline: Crude drew hard again — 3.7 mb per last week's WPSR — yet WTI settled sharply lower at $67.84, down $1.66 on the session as U.S.-Iran talks progressed and cooled the war premium. The clean-barrel split is the real story: gasoline is being pulled hard into peak driving season while distillate and jet lag, and the demand-side bid couldn't outweigh a diplomatic thaw that took the fear bid out of the tape. This is a market unwinding geopolitical risk faster than tightening fundamentals can support it. Crude & products: The latest EIA report, for the week ending June 26, 2026, showed commercial crude drawing 3.7 mb to 408.4 mb, with refinery utilization firm at 96.6% (+0.5 pts) and crude inputs at 17.2 mb/d. Gasoline drew 2.3 mb to 214.0 mb heading into the July driving window, while distillate built 2.5 mb to 108.6 mb and jet added 1.7 mb to 48.0 mb — diesel and jet demand clearly trailing gasoline. Total petroleum stocks slipped 1.9 mb to 778.9 mb. Cushing ticked up 0.71 mb to 19.67 mb but remains firmly below the ~25 mb operational floor — the hub has been operating below tank-bottom minimums for weeks (22.4 mb on May 29 down to 18.96 mb on June 19), and this week's uptick offers marginal relief without restoring functional deliverability. Refining margins: Product cracks stayed rich even as the crude complex sold off. The 3-2-1 crack sits at $59.25, with the distillate crack the standout at $67.13 and gasoline at $55.30 — margin economics still incentivizing the 96.6% run rate we saw in the WPSR. RBOB settled at $2.932/gal, up 4 cents, holding its bid on the gasoline draw, while ULSD eased 2 cents to $3.2136/gal as the distillate build capped diesel. The jet crack at $50.27 remains the soft spot, consistent with the 1.7 mb jet build. Curve structure: WTI holds a modest backwardation with M1-M12 at $1.19 and a near-flat M1-M2 at just $0.05, signaling prompt tightness that's fading as the risk premium bleeds out. Brent is essentially flat across the curve — M1-M12 at $0.10 and M1-M2 actually in contango at -$0.49 — a notably weaker structure than WTI. The Brent-WTI spread has compressed to just $3.10, a narrow gap that weakens the U.S. export arbitrage; with WTI trading close to Brent, the incentive to move barrels offshore fades, and to re-widen the spread and reopen the arb, WTI would need to fall further relative to Brent. The steep product-curve backwardation — RBOB M1-M12 at $23.50/bbl and ULSD at $22.25/bbl — underscores how much the front of the barrel is being pulled versus deferred. News & geopolitics: The dominant driver overnight was diplomacy: oil slipped below $72 Brent as U.S.-Iran talks advanced, unwinding the Hormuz risk premium that had underpinned the complex. Reuters-tracked reporting flags that the Iran strike is already rippling through LNG — India sees a Qatar LNG supply cut, a reminder that the conflict's second-order effects on gas are still in play even as crude de-escalates. Putin's admission of a domestic fuel shortage after weeks of Ukrainian refinery strikes keeps Russian product balances tight and supportive of global diesel, even as Saudi Aramco slashed July LPG prices on swelling supply. On the U.S. side, North Dakota operators remain cautious on drilling despite the price rise, and locked-out BP workers are picketing the Whiting, Indiana refinery amid a labor dispute — a supply-side risk to Midwest product flows worth watching. Goldman's flagging of two-way risks to its 2026 outlook captures the mood: a market caught between de-escalation on one side and stubbornly tight physical balances on the other.
